story:
Wilbur, old, stooped, and jittery from a lifetime of coffee, arrives at a drab saltbox house in a parallel universe, smoothing what hair he has and checking his crooked teeth in a brass doorknob. The machine worked. After fifty years building it—stealing palladium, welding, coding—he has finally come to find Terry, the girl who died young in his world, the girl he never let go of. He has imagined the life they were owed: a sensible sweetheart-cut diamond, a small wedding, an Italianate house near his job, pot roast, a medium-to-large dog, and Terry, copper-haired with freckles like stars. He still carries her creased photo in his billfold.

He knocks. The door opens on a woman time has changed: dyed bronze hair, lined face, thin lips. He decides cosmetics can be fixed; what matters is that she is alive. “Terry—” he breathes. She sneers. “Go to hell,” she says, and slams the door.

Shaken, Wilbur tracks down his counterpart—Wilbur Beta—in a pub. Beta, more amused than surprised by the interdimensional reveal, admits the obvious: he and Terry married, he got bored, he cheated, she found out, and now she despises him. The bartender nods: she hates his guts. This Terry’s rage isn’t mystery; it’s memory.

Wilbur recalibrates for another universe. Terry Gamma opens the door warm and cordial—until a cheerful, pot-bellied man slips an arm around her. She long ago moved on. Wilbur’s nose wrinkles. In his head, a true Terry wouldn’t be with anyone else. He leaves without saying goodbye.

Terry Eta’s door brings a young caretaker who bursts into tears: “You came back!” She pulls him through a musty house labeled with bright sticky notes. In a reclined twin bed lies Terry Eta, smiling lopsidedly through a stroke, calling him “my love.” Before the caretaker can settle her upright, he turns and runs.

He pins his hope to Terry Kappa. She invites him in, sets tea on an overadorned table, listens kindly as he explains he’s from a world where she died. They grew apart here, she says; her Wilbur craved grander things. Her eyes are tired but warm. She never stopped loving him. For a moment, it could be a second chance. Then Wilbur notices sriracha on the counter. Terry hated spice. Multivitamins beside it—she called them a scam. He stares at brittle hair and liver-spotted skin, and the myth he has nursed for half a century cracks. He apologizes, but really he blames her for not being his Terry, and he leaves.

At last there is Terry Nu. Copper hair bouncing, freckles bright, voice just shy of right because he modeled it from memory. She brings him beer, calls his tinkering brilliant, says everything he makes will be “out of sight.” He thinks about pulling her larynx modulator and tries not to think at all. She was made to follow him to bed, to laugh on cue, to stay. Her hand is perfectly warm in his. Everything is exactly as he programmed it to be—no cheating past, no messy autonomy, no aging, no difference. He has engineered devotion so precise it can never disappoint.

That night he lies beside her and feels nothing. He has crossed universes to chase a memory and, when real women did not fit it—angry, partnered, ill, changed—he built one that did. The machine works. He is here, and Terry is everywhere, except in the place where love should be.
